title: Knife Owners’ Protection Act of 2025
stage_label: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 13 - 10.
plain_summary: This bill protects people who travel between states with knives. If you own a knife legally in one state and want to take it to another state where it is also legal, you can transport it without getting arrested. You must store the knife safely while traveling, unless it is an emergency knife for cutting seat belts. States and cities cannot arrest you if you follow these rules. If someone tries to arrest you anyway, you can use this law to defend yourself in court.
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 13 - 10.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Knife Owners' Protection Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Interstate Transportation of Knives
sections.text: This section explains the rules for traveling between states with knives. The word State means any of the fifty states, Washington DC, American Samoa, Guam, Puerto Rico, the Northern Mariana Islands, the US Virgin Islands, and any other US territory. The word transport includes staying overnight in hotels or other temporary places, delays caused by airlines or other carriers, stops for food and gas, car repairs, emergencies, medical treatment, and all other activities related to your trip. Transport does not include carrying knives when you plan to commit a serious crime that could get you more than one year in prison if the crime involves using force or threatening to use force against someone else, or if you know or should know that such a crime will happen during your trip. Despite any federal, state, or local law or rule, any person who is legally allowed to have knives can transport them from any place where they can legally have them to any other place where they can legally have them. This applies if the person follows these rules. For travel by car, the knives must not be easy to reach from the passenger area. If the car does not have a separate trunk or storage area, the knives must be in a locked container, locked glove box, or locked center console. For travel by airplane, the knives must be in a locked container and not in the passenger cabin where people sit. For travel by any other way including by land, water, or other methods, the knives must be in a locked container. There are special rules for emergency knives. Any knife or tool made to help people escape in emergencies that has a blunt tip or protected blade for cutting seat belts can be carried in the passenger area of a car. These emergency knives do not need to be locked up. However, this exception does not apply to airplane travel where passengers go through airport security screening by the Transportation Security Administration. When staying overnight in temporary places like hotels, knives can be kept accessible as long as you follow the other transport rules. A person who is transporting knives according to these rules cannot be arrested or detained for violating any state or local knife laws unless police have probable cause to believe the person is not following the requirements in this section. A person can use this law as a legal defense in any court case. If someone uses this law as a defense in a criminal case, the state or local government must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the person was not following the requirements. Anyone who violates a person's rights under this law while acting under government authority can be sued by the person whose rights were violated. When someone uses this law as a claim or defense, the court must award reasonable attorney fees to the winning party, unless the winning party is a government or government employee. Nothing in this section limits any rights people have under state law to possess, carry, or transport knives. Nothing in this section creates additional restrictions on air travel with knives.
